request.question: Which mountain range, found in eastern North America, was seen as the western frontier prior to about 1800?

title: Hemlock woolly adelgid
Appalachian Mountains
Coast Mountains
Front Range
Bashagard Mountains
Baffin Mountains
Amanita bisporigera
Western United States
Piedmont Mountains
Geological history of the Precordillera terrane
sentences: Hemlock woolly adelgid ("Adelges tsugae"), or HWA, is member of the Sternorrhyncha suborder of the Order Hemiptera and native to East Asia.
It feeds by sucking sap from hemlock and spruce trees ("Tsuga" spp.; "Picea" spp.)
In eastern North America, it is a destructive pest that gravely threatens the eastern hemlock ("Tsuga canadensis") and the Carolina hemlock ("Tsuga caroliniana").
Though the range of eastern hemlock extends north of the current range of the adelgid, it could spread to infect these northern areas as well.
Accidentally introduced to North America from Japan, HWA was first found in the eastern United States near Richmond, Virginia, in the early 1950s.
The pest has now been established in eighteen eastern states from Georgia to Massachusetts, causing widespread mortality of hemlock trees.
As of 2015, 90% of the geographic range of eastern hemlock in North America has been impacted by HWA.
The Appalachian Mountains ( , French: "les Appalaches" ), often called the Appalachians, are a system of mountains in eastern North America.
The Appalachians first formed roughly 480 million years ago during the Ordovician Period.
It once reached elevations similar to those of the Alps and the Rocky Mountains before naturally occurring erosion.
The Appalachian chain is a barrier to east-west travel, as it forms a series of alternating ridgelines and valleys oriented in opposition to most roads running east or west.

The Coast Mountains are a major mountain range in the Pacific Coast Ranges of western North America, extending from southwestern Yukon through the Alaska Panhandle and virtually all of the Coast of British Columbia south to the Fraser River.
The mountain range's name derives from its proximity to the sea coast, and it is often referred to as the Coast Range.
The range includes volcanic and non-volcanic mountains and the extensive ice fields of the Pacific and Boundary Ranges, and the northern end of the volcanic system known as the Cascade Volcanoes.

The Coast Mountains are part of a larger mountain system called the Pacific Coast Ranges or the Pacific Mountain System, which includes the Cascade Range, the Insular Mountains, the Olympic Mountains, the Oregon Coast Range, the California Coast Ranges, the Saint Elias Mountains and the Chugach Mountains.

The Coast Mountains are also part of the American Cordilleraa Spanish term for an extensive chain of mountain rangesthat consists of an almost continuous sequence of mountain ranges that form the western backbone of North America, Central America, South America and Antarctica.

The Front Range is a mountain range of the Southern Rocky Mountains of North America located in the central portion of the U.S. State of Colorado, and southeastern portion of the U.S. State of Wyoming.
It is the first mountain range encountered moving west along the 40th parallel north across the Great Plains of North America.
The Front Range runs north-south between Casper, Wyoming and Pueblo, Colorado and rises nearly 10,000 feet above the Great Plains.
Longs Peak, Mount Evans, and Pikes Peak are its most prominent peaks, visible from the Interstate 25 corridor.
The area is a popular destination for mountain biking, hiking, climbing, and camping during the warmer months and for skiing and snowboarding during winter.
Millions of years ago the present-day Front Range was home to ancient mountain ranges, deserts, beaches, and even oceans.
The Bashagard Mountains or the Bashagerd Mountains (also known as Bashagird or Bashakerd) is a mountain range that is located in southeast of Iran.

The mountain range runs in an arc almost in a northwest-southeast direction from eastern parts of Hormozgan Province along the border with Kerman Province and stretching into southern part of Sistan and Baluchestan Province.

With an elevation of 2185 metres, Mount Buniken (Kuh-e Buniken) is the highest point of the range located in the western section of the Bashagard Mountains, east of Sardasht in Bashagard County in Hormozgan Province, and in the vicinity of a place that marks the borders of Kerman, Hormozgan, and Sistan and Baluchestan provinces.
Made of ophiolite, an assemblage of mafic igneous rocks representing remnants of former oceanic crust, the mountain range was formed finally in the Miocene and the Pliocene during the Alpine orogeny.
The mountain range is located in a region with a hot semi-desert climate.
The Baffin Mountains are a mountain range running along the northeastern coast of Baffin Island and Bylot Island, Nunavut are part of the Arctic Cordillera.
The ice-capped mountains are some of the highest peaks of eastern North America, reaching a height of 1525 - above sea level.

While they could be considered a single mountain range as they are separated by bodies of water to make Baffin Island, this is not true, as they are closely related to the other mountain ranges that make the much larger Arctic Cordillera mountain range.
Amanita bisporigera is a deadly poisonous species of fungus in the family Amanitaceae.

It is commonly known as the eastern North American destroying angel or just as the destroying angel, although the fungus shares this latter name with three other lethal white "Amanita" species, "A. ocreata", "A. verna" and "A. virosa".

The fruit bodies are found on the ground in mixed coniferous and deciduous forests of eastern North America south to Mexico, but are rare in western North America; the fungus has also been found in pine plantations in Colombia.
The mushroom has a smooth white cap that can reach up to 10 cm across, and a stipe, up to 14 cm long by 1.8 cm thick, that has a delicate white skirt-like ring near the top.
The bulbous stipe base is covered with a membranous sac-like volva.
The white gills are free from attachment to the stalk and crowded closely together.
As the species name suggests, "A. bisporigera" typically bears two spores on the basidia, although this characteristic is not as immutable as was once thought.
The Western United States, commonly referred to as the American West, the Far West, or simply the West, traditionally refers to the region comprising the westernmost states of the United States.
Because European settlement in the U.S. expanded westward after its founding, the meaning of the West has evolved over time.
Prior to about 1800, the crest of the Appalachian Mountains was seen as the western frontier.
The Piedmont Mountains are outlying mountains, sometimes called “low mountains”, that typically occur in the western Piedmont near the Blue Ridge.

Most of the features within the Piedmont physiographic region of North America lie either on the eastern border where the plateau plunges onto the Coastal Plain at the Fall Line, in the broad valleys of the river systems, or on the western border where Piedmont Mountains likely occur.

Occasionally, due to diverse rock formations, folds and outcroppings, these mountains can rise at various locations across the Piedmont like the Uwharrie Mountain Range in North Carolina or the Pine Mountain Range in Georgia.
Most of these mountains, or hills, are what is left of ancient eroded mountains.
Some, like Stone Mountain in Georgia, are solitary rock domes called Monadnocks which become further exposed with erosion.
The Piedmont is part of the greater Appalachian Mountain Range and is also referred to as the Appalachian Plateau.
The French definition of piedmont in itself translates as foothill; however, a Piedmont Mountain may be that of greater significance or prominent elevation.
The Precordillera terrane of western Argentina is a large mountain range located southeast of the main Andes mountain range.
The evolution of the Precordillera is noted for its unique formation history compared to the region nearby.

The Cambrian-Ordovian sedimentology in the Precordillera terrane has its source neither from old Andes nor nearby country rock, but shares similar characteristics with the Grenville orogeny of eastern North America.
This indicates a rift-drift history of the Precordillera in the early Paleozoic.
The Precordillera is a moving micro-continent which started from the southeast part of the ancient continent Laurentia (current location: North American plate).
The separation of the Precordillera (also named Cuyania) started around the early Cambrian.
The mass collided with Gondwana (the ancient supercontinent in the southern hemisphere) around Late Ordovician period.
Different models and thinking of rift-drift process and the time of occurrence have been proposed.
This page focuses on the evidence of drifting found in the stratigraphical record of the Precordillera, as well as exhibiting models of how the Precordillera drifted to Gondwana.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Which mountain range, found in eastern North America, was seen as the western frontier prior to about 1800?
hop1_docs: Western United States | The Western United States, commonly referred to as the American West, the Far West, or simply the West, traditionally refers to the region comprising the westernmost states of the United States.  Because European settlement in the U.S. expanded westward after its founding, the meaning of the West has evolved over time.  Prior to about 1800, the crest of the Appalachian Mountains was seen as the western frontier.

Chuska Mountains | The Chuska Mountains are an elongate range on the southeast Colorado Plateau and within the Navajo Nation whose highest elevations approach 10,000 feet.  The range is about 80 by 15 km (50 by 10 miles).  It trends north-northwest and is crossed by the state line between Arizona and New Mexico.  The highlands are a dissected plateau, with an average elevation of about 2740 m , and subdued topography.  The highest point is Roof Butte (36.4601° N, 109.0929° W) at 2994 m , near the northern end of the range in Arizona.  Other high points include the satellite Beautiful Mountain at 2861 m and Lukachukai Mountains at 2885 m , both also near the northern end, and Matthews Peak at 2911 m .  The San Juan Basin borders the Chuskas on the east, and typical elevations in nearby parts of that basin are near 1800 m .  The eastern escarpment of the mountains is marked by slumps and landslides that extend out onto the western margin of the San Juan Basin.  To the north, the Chuskas are separated from the Carrizo Mountains by Red Rock Valley, which is today commonly referred to as Red Valley.

Rocky Mountain elk | The Rocky Mountain elk ("Cervus canadensis nelsoni") is a subspecies of elk found in the Rocky Mountains and adjacent ranges of Western North America.  The winter ranges are most common in open forests and floodplain marshes in the lower elevations.  In the summer it migrates to the subalpine forests and alpine basins.  The total wild population is about one million individuals.

American Cordillera | The American Cordillera is a chain of mountain ranges (cordilleras) that consists of an almost continuous sequence of mountain ranges that form the western "backbone" of North America, Central America, South America and Antarctica.  It is also the backbone of the volcanic arc that forms the eastern half of the Pacific Ring of Fire.

Coast Mountains | The Coast Mountains are a major mountain range in the Pacific Coast Ranges of western North America, extending from southwestern Yukon through the Alaska Panhandle and virtually all of the Coast of British Columbia south to the Fraser River.  The mountain range's name derives from its proximity to the sea coast, and it is often referred to as the Coast Range.  The range includes volcanic and non-volcanic mountains and the extensive ice fields of the Pacific and Boundary Ranges, and the northern end of the volcanic system known as the Cascade Volcanoes.  The Coast Mountains are part of a larger mountain system called the Pacific Coast Ranges or the Pacific Mountain System, which includes the Cascade Range, the Insular Mountains, the Olympic Mountains, the Oregon Coast Range, the California Coast Ranges, the Saint Elias Mountains and the Chugach Mountains.  The Coast Mountains are also part of the American Cordilleraa Spanish term for an extensive chain of mountain rangesthat consists of an almost continuous sequence of mountain ranges that form the western backbone of North America, Central America, South America and Antarctica.

Rhododendron occidentale | Rhododendron occidentale, the western azalea or California azalea, is one of two deciduous "Rhododendron" species native to western North America (the other is "Rhododendron albiflorum").  The western azalea is known to occur as far north as Lincoln and Douglas Counties in Oregon and as far south as the mountains of San Diego county.  Typically found in the coastal ranges of western North America, it also grows in the Cascade and Sierra Nevada mountain ranges, but is not known east of them.

Arbutus xalapensis | Arbutus xalapensis, commonly known as the Texas madrone, naked Indian tree or Texas madroño, is a species of flowering plant in the heather family.  It is native to Central America, the southwestern United States (western Texas and New Mexico), and throughout Mexico.  It is found in canyons and mountains, on rocky plains, and in oak woodlands, at altitudes of up to 3,000 m (10,000 feet) in the south of the range, but lower, down to 600 m (1800 feet) in the north of the range.
response.summary_1: Passage [1] states "the crest of the Appalachian Mountains was seen as the western frontier" (this supports the Appalachian Mountains as the frontier). Missing/needed verification: confirm that the Appalachian Mountains are a mountain range "found in eastern North America." Retrieval direction/anchors: fetch an authoritative article on "Appalachian Mountains" (search: "Appalachian Mountains eastern North America" or the "Appalachian Mountains" Wikipedia page) that explicitly says the range is in eastern North America.
hop2_docs: Cove (Appalachian Mountains) | In the central and southern Appalachian Mountains of Eastern North America, a cove is a small valley between two ridge lines that is closed at one or both ends.

Appalachian Mountains | The Appalachian Mountains ( , French: "les Appalaches" ), often called the Appalachians, are a system of mountains in eastern North America.  The Appalachians first formed roughly 480 million years ago during the Ordovician Period.  It once reached elevations similar to those of the Alps and the Rocky Mountains before naturally occurring erosion.  The Appalachian chain is a barrier to east-west travel, as it forms a series of alternating ridgelines and valleys oriented in opposition to most roads running east or west.

Long Range Mountains | The Long Range Mountains are a series of mountains along the west coast of the Canadian island of Newfoundland.  They also form the northernmost section of the Appalachian chain on the eastern seaboard of North America.  In 2003 it was announced that the International Appalachian Trail would be extended through the Long Range Mountains.

Northern Appalachians Seismic Zone | The Northern Appalachians Seismic Zone is an active seismic zone in the Appalachian Mountains of eastern North America, extending from New Brunswick southwards into New England and Boston.  Both the seismic zone and the resultant Appalachian Mountains were created by the collision of two tectonic plates about 440–480 million years ago during the middle Ordovician Period.

Mount Mitchell | Mount Mitchell is the highest peak of the Appalachian Mountains and the highest peak in mainland eastern North America.  It is located near Burnsville in Yancey County, North Carolina, in the Black Mountain subrange of the Appalachians, and about 19 mi northeast of Asheville.  It is protected by Mount Mitchell State Park and surrounded by the Pisgah National Forest.  Mount Mitchell's elevation is 6684 ft above sea level.

Galium latifolium | Galium latifolium (purple bedstraw) is a North American species of plants in the madder family.  It is native to eastern North America, primarily the Appalachian Mountains from Pennsylvania to Alabama.  There are also a few lowland populations in eastern Maryland and eastern South Carolina.

Great Appalachian Valley | The Great Valley, also called the Great Appalachian Valley or Great Valley Region, is one of the major landform features of eastern North America.  It is a gigantic trough—a chain of valley lowlands—and the central feature of the Appalachian Mountain system.  The trough stretches about 1200 mi from Quebec to Alabama and has been an important north-south route of travel since prehistoric times.
response.summary_2: The crest of the Appalachian Mountains was seen as the western frontier prior to about 1800. The Appalachian Mountains are a system of mountains in eastern North America, which corroborates the context claim.
